trajectory_summary: James is one of the most self-motivated customers in the corpus — he called back multiple times, volunteered his usage drivers unprompted, and booked without resistance. The rep executed the procedural skeleton cleanly (verify, bill collection, booking) but treated a warm, high-intent lead as a form to process. Three consecutive enthusiasm signals went unanchored. The appointment is booked but the closer walks in with no emotional foundation built — James's motivation is real, but the rep didn't deepen it.
outcome_dispute: The appointment was booked and is structurally sound for tomorrow (April 15, 5:00 PM). However, the button-up was partial — one_hour_block_stated, no_driving_at_computer, and is_that_fair_handshake were all skipped. The sit is real but the show-rate anchors are missing. Outcome tag should reflect 'booked, partial button-up' rather than a clean sit.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill anchor flip — converting enthusiasm signals into emotional alignment
primary_coaching_focus.quote: See, from June to the October I'm running roughly over a thousand kilowatts per hour.
primary_coaching_focus.why: James volunteered three consecutive load-increase signals (pool, 1,000+ kWh usage, Jacuzzi coming back online) and the rep responded with 'Mhmm' each time. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture is necessary but not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. James did the rep's work for her and she didn't take the handoff. This is the universal floor gap the schema identifies, and this call is a clean example because the customer made it easy. Use it to show Alice what a customer-led flip looks like and what the rep's job is when the customer hands it to them.

key_moments.quote: Now we were thinking about adding a pool, which is one of the reasons I wanna go with solar.
key_moments.stewart_read: James volunteers a planned pool addition as an explicit motivation for solar — this is a load-increase signal and an emotional anchor in one line. The rep's response ('I'll just put that in the notes for you') is procedurally defensible under setter_scope_discipline: the rep isn't wrong to defer pool-sizing specifics to the specialist. However, the strategic read would have the rep acknowledge the emotional driver — 'that's exactly why people go solar before a pool, the load jump is significant' — before deferring the technical detail. The reactive read is that the rep heard 'pool' as a logistics note rather than as the customer's stated reason for calling. The reactive read is better supported: the rep deferred the signal entirely rather than anchoring on the motivation and deferring only the technical sizing.

James explains the $7 bill is a two-day partial bill from a utility switch, not his real usage. The rep's instinct to laugh and engage ('$7 for a whole month? That sounds outrageous') is a positive rapport beat — she reads the absurdity correctly and James responds warmly ('I wouldn't be calling you if that was the case'). This is a small but genuine connection moment. The rep then correctly pivots to pulling the actual usage history. Worth reinforcing as a positive instinct.

key_moments.quote: See, from June to the October I'm running roughly over a thousand kilowatts per hour.
key_moments.stewart_read: James reads his own usage data aloud — 1,000+ kWh from June through October — and the rep's response is 'Mhmm.' This is the bill-anchor moment. The data is in hand, the customer is narrating it, and the rep offers no emotional response. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into alignment-against-the-utility. The rep had the number, the customer's voice, and the context (Jacuzzi offline, pool coming) to make this land. The 'Mhmm' is a procedural acknowledgment, not an anchor.

key_moments.quote: Oh, that's that's nothing. This is without my my my Jacuzzi this year. I didn't turn it on because I had a leak. But I've got it fixed now, so I already know that that that deal is gonna go up.
key_moments.stewart_read: James is doing the rep's emotional work — he's projecting his own bill pain forward and narrating why it will get worse. This is the third consecutive load-increase signal in two minutes (pool at 03:54, 1,000 kWh at 05:49, Jacuzzi at 05:58). The rep responds 'Mhmm.' James has handed the rep a bill flip on a silver platter three times. The rep's consistent minimal acknowledgment is not strategic patience — it's a pattern of not recognizing enthusiasm signals as leverage.
